did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep convolutional neural networks (ResNet152, InceptionResNet-V2, MobileNet) and an ensemble model with transfer learning and specific loss functions (categorical cross-entropy, squared hinge) to classify five chronic skin conditions (acne, eczema, actinic keratosis, rosacea, and basal cell carcinoma) from dermatoscopic images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a deep learning framework using ResNet152, InceptionResNet-V2, MobileNet, and an ensemble model to classify five chronic skin conditions. It includes a proposed methodology, dataset description, experimental setup with specific hardware (NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1080 Ti), and reports new quantitative results (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) for the proposed models, distinguishing it from a review paper despite containing a literature review section.

title: IDENTIFICATION OF DIFFERENT CHRONIC SKIN CONDITIONS USING DEEP CONVOLUTIONAL NEURAL NETWORKS
journal: 2023 International Conference on Advancement in Computation & Computer Technologies (InCACCT)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of diagnosing five chronic skin conditions—acne, eczema, actinic keratosis, rosacea, and basal cell carcinoma—using deep convolutional neural networks. The authors utilized a dataset of over 10,000 dermatoscopic images, applying data augmentation to balance the classes and resizing images to 224x224 pixels. The research evaluated and fine-tuned several transfer learning models, including ResNet152, InceptionResNet-V2, MobileNet, and an ensemble model, using categorical cross-entropy and squared hinge loss functions. Experiments were conducted on an NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1080 Ti GPU with a training set of 7,224 images and a validation set of 1,255 images. The MobileNet model achieved the highest performance, attaining an accuracy of 97.05%, sensitivity of 97.91%, and specificity of 98.12%. The results demonstrate that the proposed deep learning framework effectively classifies skin diseases with superior accuracy compared to the other tested architectures.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the five chronic skin conditions studied in the classification task using a dataset of over 10,000 dermatoscopic images.

all_task_types.target: Five chronic skin conditions (acne, eczema, actinic keratosis, rosacea, and basal cell carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of dermatoscopic images into five discrete diagnostic categories using deep convolutional neural networks (ResNet152, InceptionResNet-V2, MobileNet, and an Ensemble model). The model predicts discrete disease labels using categorical cross-entropy and squared hinge loss functions, with performance evaluated via accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.

datasets_used.name: Custom Dermoscopic Skin Disease Dataset
datasets_used.description: A collection of dermatoscopic images comprising five different chronic skin conditions. The dataset was selected to provide a holistic solution for skin disease classification and was processed by resizing images to 224x224 pixels and applying data augmentation to balance class distribution.

use.training: Used for training the deep convolutional neural network models (ResNet152, InceptionResNet-V2, MobileNet, and Ensemble) with data augmentation applied to balance the skewed class distribution.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process to monitor performance and loss.
use.testing: The paper mentions using 1255 images for validation and 7224 for training in the conclusion, and states in the experimental setup that '12% of the training data are utilized for evaluation' and '19% as a testing set', but does not provide a distinct, separate test set count or final test results for the proposed models. The reported accuracy/sensitivity/specificity metrics appear to be based on the validation set or a hold-out portion of the training data.
use.other: Data augmentation was applied to this dataset to create extra pictures by rotating or transforming current data to address class imbalance.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To summarize the performance of the respective models and calculate accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.
To evaluate and compare the classification performance of ResNet152, InceptionResNet-V2, Ensemble Model, and MobileNet.
To balance the skewed class distribution in the dataset by creating extra pictures through rotation or transformation.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics derived from confusion matrices.

Custom Dermoscopic Skin Disease Dataset.notes: MobileNet achieved the highest performance among the tested models. The dataset consisted of over 10,000 images divided into training (7,224) and validation (1,255) sets. Data augmentation was applied to balance class distribution. The MobileNet model was identified as the proposed model with the best accuracy.

architecture_used.main_model: ResNet152, InceptionResNet-V2, MobileNet, and an Ensemble Model

ResNet152.description: Residual network with residual learning units and shortcut connections to prevent vanishing gradients. Trained on ImageNet.

InceptionResNet-V2.description: 164-layer network combining Inception blocks (blending different-sized convolutional filters) with residual connections. Evolved from Residual link and Inception structure.

MobileNet.description: Lightweight CNN using depth-wise separable convolutions (1x1 point-wise creases and 3x3 depth-wise creases) to reduce parameters and computation time.

Ensemble Model.description: Aggregates predictions from base CNN models (Level-0) to create a single overall prediction (Level-1).

training_details.optimizer_framework: TensorFlow backend with Keras API; Fast AI used for implementation
training_details.hardware: NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1080 Ti GPU (8GB CUDA core)
loss_functions: Squared Hinge loss (for ResNet152 and InceptionResNet-V2)
training_details.data_augmentation: Rotation and transformation to balance class distribution
training_details.feature_extraction: 128-D traits extracted for ResNet152 tuning duration measurement

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Probability distribution across 5 input classes (Acne, Eczema, Actinic Keratosis, Rosacea, Basal Cell Carcinoma)
classification_or_output_head.loss_for_evaluation: Cross-Entropy Function used after Softmax for Ensemble

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to 224x224 pixels to avoid training errors
Data augmentation (rotation and transformation) to balance skewed class distribution
Layer-wise fine-tuning of pre-trained deep CNN models (ResNet152, InceptionResNet-V2) instead of block-wise adjustment
Utilization of transfer learning from the ImageNet dataset

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to balance the skewed class distribution in the dataset by creating extra pictures from existing data. Specific probabilities, angles, or ranges were not reported.

conclusions: The proposed deep learning framework effectively classifies five chronic skin conditions (acne, eczema, actinic keratosis, rosacea, and basal cell carcinoma) using dermatoscopic images.
MobileNet achieved the highest performance among the tested models, attaining an accuracy of 97.05%, sensitivity of 97.91%, and specificity of 98.12%.
MobileNet outperformed ResNet152 (84.91% accuracy), InceptionResNet-V2 (87.42% accuracy), and the Ensemble Model (88.75% accuracy).
The study demonstrates that the suggested framework achieves superior accuracy in skin disease tasks compared to many existing works.
The proposed model aims to address the scarcity of skilled dermatologists and the high cost of sophisticated diagnostic machinery by providing a handy, data-driven diagnosis solution.
Data augmentation was utilized to balance the skewed class distribution in the dataset of over 10,000 images.
Transfer learning was employed to adapt pre-trained models (ResNet152, InceptionResNet-V2, MobileNet) to learn distinctive characteristics from skin condition images.
important_remarks: The dataset is small (approx. 10,000 images) and custom-collected, lacking external validation on independent, diverse dermatological datasets, which limits generalizability.

The paper reports conflicting data split descriptions: Section II states a training set of 7,224 and validation set of 1,255, while Section V mentions 68% training, 13% evaluation, and 19% testing, but does not provide distinct test set results.
No formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) is reported; performance comparisons are based solely on point estimates of accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.
Data augmentation was used to balance class distribution, but specific augmentation parameters (e.g., rotation angles, flip probabilities) are not detailed, raising reproducibility concerns.
The study focuses on five chronic skin conditions but excludes melanoma, limiting its clinical scope for critical skin cancer diagnosis tasks.
The proposed solution is conceptualized as a website for accessibility, but no information on clinical deployment, user interface, or real-world integration is provided.

Potential data leakage risk exists if the 'testing set' mentioned in Section V was not strictly held out from the training/augmentation process, as the final reported metrics appear to be on the validation set.
